# Copilot Instructions for Webshop E2E (Playwright + TypeScript)

## Purpose

Ensure maintainable, stable, and readable Playwright tests with consistent architecture (POM + fixtures). These rules apply to all code — written by hand or generated by AI.

---

## Tech Stack

- **TypeScript** + **Playwright Test**
- **Page Object Model (POM)** + custom fixtures
- Business-flow oriented tests

---

## Project Structure

```
pages/          → Full page objects (Login, Home, Cart, Checkout…)
components/     → Reusable UI blocks (Header, Menu…)
fixtures/       → Custom Playwright fixtures (extends `test`, provides `pm`)
types/          → TypeScript interfaces for test data
data/           → Test data (.ts objects and .json files)
utils/          → Helpers and utilities
tests/
  auth/
    login.spec.ts
  cart/
    cart.spec.ts
  checkout/
    checkout.spec.ts
```

- File goes directly in the feature folder — never create a subfolder matching the file name

Wrong:
tests/search/search/search.spec.ts // ❌ extra subfolder
tests/search/Search/search.spec.ts // ❌ extra subfolder

Correct:
tests/search/search.spec.ts // ✅ one file, no extra subfolder

---

## POM & Component Conventions

- One class per page/component (PascalCase)
- Constructor accepts `Page`
- Locators: **`private readonly`** at top of class — always both modifiers
- Methods: one action per method, clear naming (`clickLogin`, `setEmail`, `addToCart`)
- Optional workflow methods allowed: `login()`, `checkout()`
- **No assertions inside POM — ever**
- Components are reusable UI blocks, accessed via PageManager

### Example

```ts
export class LoginPage {
  private readonly emailInput = this.page.getByLabel('Email');
  private readonly passwordInput = this.page.getByLabel('Password');
  private readonly loginButton = this.page.getByRole('button', {
    name: 'Log in',
  });

  constructor(private readonly page: Page) {}

  async setEmail(email: string) {
    await this.emailInput.fill(email);
  }

  async setPassword(password: string) {
    await this.passwordInput.fill(password);
  }

  async clickLogin() {
    await this.loginButton.click();
  }

  async login(email: string, password: string) {
    await this.setEmail(email);
    await this.setPassword(password);
    await this.clickLogin();
  }
}
```

### PageManager

- Central access point for all pages/components
- Uses single `Page` instance
- Exposes `readonly` properties

### Fixtures

- Extend Playwright `test`, provide `pm` (PageManager)
- Re-export `expect`
- Keep typing explicit

---

## Locator Strategy

### Golden Rule

> Use the **simplest stable locator**. Never guess — always inspect actual HTML first.

### Priority Order

| Priority | Method                                                     | When to use                                                                   |
| -------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 1️⃣       | `getByRole`, `getByLabel`, `getByPlaceholder`, `getByText` | **Always prefer** — accessible, stable, user-oriented                         |
| 2️⃣       | `id`, `data-testid`, `data-test`                           | When built-in locators are too complex or element has unique stable attribute |
| 3️⃣       | Scoped `.filter()`                                         | When multiple elements match — start with built-in, narrow with filter        |
| 4️⃣       | Short CSS (1–2 levels, stable)                             | **Last resort** only                                                          |

### Examples

```ts
// ✅ Priority 1 — built-in
page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Add to cart' });

// ✅ Priority 2 — stable attribute
page.locator('[data-testid="checkout-button"]');

// ✅ Priority 3 — scoped filter
page
  .getByRole('listitem')
  .filter({ hasText: 'Product Name' })
  .getByRole('button', { name: 'Add to cart' });

// ✅ Priority 4 — short CSS (last resort)
page.locator('.product-card button.add-to-cart');
```

### Forbidden

- `nth-child`, `nth-of-type`, `.nth()`, index-based selection
- Long CSS chains (3+ levels)
- Auto-generated/dynamic classes (`.css-abc123`, `id="react-aria-123"`)
- Guessing locators without inspecting DOM

### Complexity Rule

If locator needs more than 3 chained steps → simplify with `data-testid`, short CSS, or stable ID.

### When in Doubt

- **Do NOT guess** — ask for HTML or use Playwright codegen.

---

## Test Authoring

### Structure

- One file per feature: `[feature].spec.ts`
- Use `describe` to group positive / negative scenarios
- Use shared fixtures (`pm`)
- Use `beforeEach` / `afterEach` where needed

```ts
describe('Login - positive', () => {
  test(
    'should login successfully with valid credentials',
    { tag: ['@smoke'] },
    async ({ pm }) => {
      // ...
    },
  );
});

describe('Login - negative', () => {
  test(
    'should show error when password is incorrect',
    { tag: ['@regression'] },
    async ({ pm }) => {
      // ...
    },
  );
});
```

### Test Naming

Format: `should [action] when [condition]`

- Always start with `should`
- Describe user behavior, not code (`should show error`, not `clickButton`)
- Keep under 80 characters

### Tags

Use Playwright built-in syntax (v1.42+) — tags go in options object, **never in test name**.

| Tag           | Purpose               | Run frequency |
| ------------- | --------------------- | ------------- |
| `@smoke`      | Critical happy paths  | Every deploy  |
| `@regression` | Full feature coverage | Nightly / PR  |
| `@sanity`     | Post-deploy env check | After release |
| `@api`        | API-level tests       | PR            |
| `@ui`         | UI-only tests         | PR            |

Rules:

- Every test must have at least one tag
- `@smoke` tests must never depend on other tests
- Positive scenarios → `@smoke`, negative → `@regression`

```bash
npx playwright test --grep @smoke        # smoke only
npx playwright test --grep "negative"    # negative blocks
npx playwright test auth/                # entire folder
```

### Auth vs No-Auth

- Separate test folders
- Use `storageState` for authenticated tests
- Validate auth state

### Assertions

- Use: `toBeVisible`, `toHaveText`, `toContainText`
- Avoid arbitrary waits (`waitForTimeout`)
- Assertions belong **only in test files**, never in POM

---

## Type & Data Conventions

- **No `any`** — use strong typing everywhere
- Interfaces in `types/`, data in `data/`
- No hardcoded strings in test body

### TypeScript objects (default — small, stable data)

```ts
// types/user.ts
export interface User {
  email: string;
  password: string;
  role: 'guest' | 'admin';
}

// data/users.ts
export const validUser: User = {
  email: 'test@example.com',
  password: 'Test1234!',
  role: 'guest',
};
```

### JSON files (larger datasets, parameterized scenarios)

```ts
// data/products.json
[
  { name: 'MacBook', price: 1500, quantity: 1 },
  { name: 'iPhone', price: 800, quantity: 2 },
];

// in test — use for...of (async-safe), not forEach
import products from '../data/products.json';

for (const product of products) {
  test(
    `should add ${product.name} to cart`,
    { tag: ['@regression'] },
    async ({ pm }) => {
      /* ... */
    },
  );
}
```

---

## Code Style

- `async/await` everywhere
- `readonly` where possible
- Small, focused methods
- No one-letter variables

---

## AI-Specific Rules

All rules above apply to AI-generated code. Additionally:

- **Never generate locators without inspecting actual HTML** — ask for it if not provided
- **Always refactor** Playwright codegen output before committing
- **Never break existing architecture** — follow POM, fixtures, project structure
- When unsure — ask, don't guess
